name: Conroe Independent School District
districtFact: Conroe ISD has been one of the fastest-growing school districts in Texas for over two decades — driven by the expansion of The Woodlands, one of America's most celebrated master-planned communities, and Montgomery County's continued growth as a destination for Houston-area professionals seeking alternatives to Harris County suburbs.

about.title: About Conroe Independent School District
about.content: Families moving to the Conroe or Woodlands area often assume they'll be in a local, neighborhood-sized district. What they find instead is one of the 10 largest school districts in Texas: Conroe ISD covers approximately 260 square miles of Montgomery County, serving roughly 76,000 students across more than 70 campuses. The district spans the city of Conroe (the Montgomery County seat), The Woodlands, Shenandoah, Oak Ridge North, Panorama Village, and large areas of unincorporated Montgomery County north of Houston. On the Texas Education Agency's A-F accountability system, Conroe ISD consistently earns district-level ratings of B or above, and the graduation rate has held above 95% in recent TEA reporting periods — above the Texas state average. Montgomery County ranked among the top 15 fastest-growing counties in the United States for much of the 2010s, according to U.S. Census Bureau estimates; Conroe ISD's enrollment reflects that directly, with the district opening new campuses on a near-annual basis to absorb growth in communities along the Grand Parkway and I-45 corridors. The district's footprint extends well beyond what most new residents expect — reaching south toward the Spring area along I-45 and west into communities near FM 1488. Zone lines run through subdivisions rather than along obvious geographic boundaries, and real estate listings frequently show outdated school assignments. Your actual campus is determined by your street address alone; the Conroe ISD school finder at conroeisd.net is the only reliable confirmation.

about.title: Montgomery County Growth and the Houston Commute
about.content: When developer George Mitchell opened The Woodlands in 1974, the drive to downtown Houston on a then-quiet I-45 ran under 30 minutes. Five decades of growth later, that same corridor ranks among the most congested highway segments in Texas: U.S. Census Bureau estimates show Montgomery County added more than 200,000 residents between 2000 and 2020, and the population has continued rising since. The practical result for Conroe ISD families: a 25-mile commute to downtown Houston now runs 50 to 70 minutes during school-year weekday mornings, and the dropoff window — whether a parent leaves campus at 7:45 or 8:15 — determines whether they clear the worst of I-45 or sit in it. Parents commuting to the Houston Energy Corridor, the Texas Medical Center, or downtown should budget the longer end of that range on the drive in; the return trip in the afternoon is often faster but less predictable near the Beltway 8 interchange. The Grand Parkway (SH 99) has become the preferred route for residents in western Conroe ISD communities — Woodforest, Harmony, and Harper's Preserve — bypassing I-45 entirely for workplaces further west or north of The Woodlands. George Bush Intercontinental Airport (IAH) sits approximately 20 miles south of The Woodlands, notably closer than it appears when navigating the Houston metro, and is the relevant airport for almost all international flights and most major domestic routes from the district. For Spring Break travel, Conroe ISD's break often falls one to two weeks before Houston ISD's — in those years, IAH departure demand during Conroe ISD's window is split rather than concentrated against Harris County spring break traffic. That growth context explains two things Conroe ISD families learn quickly: school zones are not static, and new campuses opening across the district regularly trigger redistricting that affects established neighborhoods as well as newly developed ones.

about.title: School Performance Across Conroe ISD
about.content: Conroe ISD's most important internal reality for families evaluating schools: performance varies significantly between campuses, and the variation tracks closely with community income levels. The Woodlands High School and Woodlands College Park High School serve one of the highest-median-income communities in Texas and consistently appear near the top of statewide rankings compiled by US News & World Report and TEA accountability summaries — both campuses have strong AP participation rates and college-going rates that are well above the Texas statewide average. Conroe High School and other campuses serving the city of Conroe and lower-income portions of the district serve a broader socioeconomic range and show more typical large-district performance profiles. District-wide, the TEA's official report card shows Conroe ISD earning mostly B and above ratings on the state's A-F accountability system, with a district graduation rate above 95% in recent reporting — above the Texas statewide average. The teacher-student ratio across Conroe ISD runs approximately 15:1 at the district level, consistent with large Texas ISDs of similar size. Families making housing decisions within Conroe ISD specifically because of school performance should review TEA's individual campus report cards at tea.texas.gov rather than relying on district-level ratings; campus variation across the district's 260-square-mile footprint is meaningful. For high school selection: assigned campus is geography-dependent, so verifying which high school a specific address feeds is the starting point for any campus comparison.

about.title: Enrollment: What to Know Before You Arrive
about.content: Texas kindergarten enrollment requires proof that a child turns 5 on or before September 1 — earlier than the October 1 or December 1 cutoffs common in other states, and a detail that catches relocating families off guard more than almost any other enrollment requirement. Required documents include proof of Montgomery County residence, birth certificate, Texas-specific immunization records, and prior school records for transferring students. Texas immunization requirements differ from most other states; if your records were kept in a different format, a physician visit to update them before enrollment day prevents delays. If a student has an IEP or 504 plan from another district, bring the full paperwork — Conroe ISD is required to honor existing accommodations on enrollment without a waiting period. Kindergarten registration typically opens in spring for the following fall; popular campuses in The Woodlands can fill quickly, so watch conroeisd.net for the registration window rather than waiting until summer. Mid-year transfers — common in Conroe ISD given the energy sector employment cycles that drive January enrollment waves — are processed on a rolling basis through the district Enrollment Center.

content: Conroe ISD's Spring Break is often one to two weeks earlier than Houston ISD's — in years when the breaks diverge, IAH flights and Galveston cruises during Conroe ISD's break week may have more availability than during the Harris County spring break window. Check both districts' calendars each year before booking family travel; the dates don't reliably align.

The district's calendar lists 'Student Holidays' scattered throughout the fall and winter. These are no-school days for students even though staff report to campus. They don't appear on standard federal holiday calendars and are easy to miss — especially the pairs that fall on a Friday and the following Monday, creating accidental four-day weekends. Download the official calendar and mark these in your calendar before September.

Conroe ISD redistricts frequently as new campuses open to relieve overcrowding. If you are buying or renting in a specific neighborhood because of a school assignment, confirm the current zone at conroeisd.net and ask the district whether redistricting is anticipated before signing a lease or closing on a home. Zones have changed mid-year in some cases.

Lake Conroe and surrounding camps fill quickly after Conroe ISD's last day in May. Popular waterfront programs, marina-based activities, and recreational camps book months in advance — register immediately after Spring Break rather than waiting until the school year ends. The week following the last day is one of the busiest on the lake.

compareIntro: Conroe ISD is the dominant school district in Montgomery County and the primary option for families living north of Houston in The Woodlands, Conroe, and surrounding communities. Its calendar is set independently from Harris County districts — including Houston ISD, Klein ISD, and Spring ISD — meaning Spring Break dates, first-day timing, and Student Holiday placement can differ by a week or more in any given year. The most practically significant calendar difference families encounter is Spring Break timing: Conroe ISD frequently breaks earlier than Houston ISD, which affects family travel coordination across the Greater Houston area.

districtFaqs.q: Is The Woodlands in Conroe ISD or a separate school district?
districtFaqs.a: The majority of The Woodlands, Texas is within Conroe Independent School District's attendance boundaries — there is no 'Woodlands ISD.' The Woodlands High School and Woodlands College Park High School are both Conroe ISD campuses. A small number of addresses near the southern and eastern edges of The Woodlands may fall in Klein ISD or Spring ISD; the only reliable way to confirm is to use the Conroe ISD school finder at conroeisd.net with your specific street address. Real estate listings and neighborhood guides sometimes show incorrect school assignments, so verify directly before committing to a neighborhood or home.

districtFaqs.q: Does Conroe ISD have gifted and talented or magnet programs?
districtFaqs.a: Conroe ISD's gifted and talented program is called PACE — Program of Academic and Creative Enrichment — and serves students from first grade through twelfth grade. Referrals can come from teachers, parents, or the students themselves; the district uses a multi-criteria assessment process rather than a single IQ cutoff for placement. At the elementary level, PACE typically takes the form of pullout enrichment sessions; at the secondary level, it integrates with honors, Pre-AP, and AP course sequences. Conroe ISD does not operate a centralized magnet school the way Houston ISD does — advanced offerings are distributed across campuses, meaning a student's access to specific programs depends partly on which campus they're assigned to. Dual-credit courses through Lone Star College are available at Conroe ISD high schools, providing college credit before graduation. Families who specifically want gifted services should ask each campus directly about PACE availability and current enrollment capacity, since program depth varies across the district's 73 campuses.

districtFaqs.q: What are 'Student Holidays' in Conroe ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Student Holidays are official no-school days for students, typically used for teacher professional development, grading, or campus preparation. Conroe ISD schedules several Student Holidays throughout the year — often in pairs in October and November — that fall outside the standard federal holiday calendar. These days require the same childcare planning as any other school holiday. The specific Student Holiday dates shift from year to year, so families should download the official calendar at the start of each school year and mark all Student Holidays before September rather than discovering them a day or two in advance.

districtFaqs.q: Does Conroe ISD have school on Good Friday?
districtFaqs.a: It depends on the school year. In 2025-2026, Conroe ISD observes both Good Friday (April 3) and Easter Monday (April 6) as Student Holidays, creating a four-day Easter break — an arrangement less common among major Texas ISDs. In 2026-2027, neither Good Friday nor Easter Monday appears as a scheduled holiday on the official calendar. This is one of the most important calendar differences to verify year to year for families with Easter travel plans, since the Easter break structure changes significantly between the two school years.

districtFaqs.q: Does Conroe ISD have a fall break?
districtFaqs.a: Conroe ISD does not schedule a dedicated fall break week. The fall semester runs continuously from the August start through Thanksgiving, with isolated Student Holidays in October and November providing occasional single days or two-day stretches without school. Thanksgiving Break covers the full school week (Monday through Friday before Thanksgiving). Families expecting a structured October fall break — common in some other states and districts — should review the specific year's calendar for the Student Holiday dates that serve that function in Conroe ISD.

districtFaqs.q: What time does school start and end in Conroe ISD?
districtFaqs.a: Bell schedules in Conroe ISD vary by campus level, and high school start times are often earlier than families coming from other states expect. Elementary, middle, and high school bell times differ by campus and are posted on individual school pages at conroeisd.net at the start of each school year. Conroe ISD generally follows the Texas pattern of early high school starts — sometimes as early as 7:15 to 7:30 a.m. — which affects UIL athletic practice scheduling, bus pickup windows, and morning family routines before the first week of school begins. August start dates coincide with peak Montgomery County heat, so UIL heat protocols can modify outdoor practice schedules during the first several weeks of the school year regardless of bell time. Families moving from states where high schools begin at 8:30 or 9:00 a.m. consistently find early Texas start times one of the bigger practical adjustments of their first year. Confirm your specific campus's bell schedule directly on conroeisd.net before summer ends — times can shift when the district adjusts bus routes or modifies campus staffing ahead of a new year.

districtFaqs.q: Does Conroe ISD close for hurricanes and tropical storms?
districtFaqs.a: Yes. Conroe ISD follows Montgomery County emergency management protocols when tropical weather threatens the Greater Houston area. Montgomery County is far enough inland to reduce direct wind damage risk compared to coastal communities, but hurricane remnants and associated flooding regularly affect roads and school operations across the region. The district closes in coordination with county emergency officials and announces closures via the CISD website, the CISD mobile app, and local television stations (KHOU, KPRC, ABC13). Under Texas Education Agency guidelines, districts can designate several weather-related closure days without mandatory makeup; extended closures may require added days at the end of the school year. Families new to the area should sign up for Conroe ISD emergency notifications and Montgomery County OEM alerts before the August school start — peak Atlantic hurricane season runs August through October, overlapping directly with the first weeks of school.

districtFaqs.q: What are the Conroe ISD school bus eligibility requirements?
districtFaqs.a: Conroe ISD provides bus transportation for students who live more than two miles from their assigned campus — the threshold set by Texas state law for school district transportation. Students within two miles are generally not eligible for district bus service. Conroe ISD's 260-square-mile footprint means a large share of students do qualify, particularly those in outer communities along I-45 and the Grand Parkway corridors; but families in subdivisions built close to newly opened campuses — a recurring situation as the district adds schools to absorb growth — may fall inside the two-mile cutoff and find they are not on a bus route. Bus routes are published on the Conroe ISD website at the start of each school year and can change when new campuses open or redistricting occurs. Families should verify bus eligibility for their specific address through the Conroe ISD transportation department rather than estimating from a map; route changes after redistricting are among the most common sources of confusion for families who assumed prior-year routes would continue.

livingHere.intro: Montgomery County north of Houston has transformed over the past three decades from a rural timber and agricultural area into one of the fastest-growing suburban regions in the United States. The Woodlands anchored that growth, but development has spread across Conroe, Woodforest, Harmony, and the Grand Parkway corridor. For families new to the area, a few local realities shape how the school calendar intersects with daily life.

highlights.label: The Woodlands
highlights.detail: One of America's most recognized master-planned communities, The Woodlands is fully within Conroe ISD. The Woodlands Town Center, Waterway, and Cynthia Woods Mitchell Pavilion (outdoor amphitheater) are the social center of the district — concerts at the Pavilion on school nights affect pickup traffic noticeably.

highlights.label: Lake Conroe
highlights.detail: A large recreational reservoir north of Conroe with marinas, lakefront camps, and water sport facilities. Summer camp programs tied to the lake fill within days of the Conroe ISD school year ending — register immediately after Spring Break.

highlights.label: I-45 Commute Corridor
highlights.detail: The primary route between Montgomery County and Houston. School-year rush hours can push a 25-mile commute past 60 minutes. Families arriving from other metros are often surprised by how significantly traffic patterns shift between summer and the school year — especially at the I-45/Beltway 8 interchange.

highlights.label: George Bush Intercontinental Airport (IAH)
highlights.detail: About 20 miles south of The Woodlands, IAH is the practical airport for most Conroe ISD families. Departure demand during Conroe ISD's Spring Break week is high — book holiday and spring break flights well before January.

highlights.label: Energy Sector Employment
highlights.detail: A large share of Conroe ISD families include professionals working in Houston's oil and gas, petrochemical, and energy services industries. Layoff cycles and corporate relocation from energy companies produce mid-year enrollment waves — Conroe ISD's Enrollment Center processes transfers year-round, and new student influxes in January are common.

highlights.label: Montgomery County Fair
highlights.detail: Held annually in October, the Montgomery County Fair is one of the largest in Texas and draws attendance from across the region. It overlaps with the school calendar in mid-October, near the Student Holiday cluster — families often plan fair visits around those days off.

highlights.label: Hurricane and Tropical Storm Season
highlights.detail: Runs June through November, with peak risk in August and September — the same weeks as back-to-school. Montgomery County is inland enough to reduce direct landfall risk compared to coastal areas, but tropical storm remnants and flooding regularly affect roads and school operations. Conroe ISD follows Montgomery County emergency management for closure decisions.

highlights.label: Grand Parkway (SH 99) Growth Corridor
highlights.detail: The completed segments of the Grand Parkway through western Montgomery County have opened large new residential areas to development. Families in newer Woodforest, Harmony, and Harper's Preserve communities are entirely within Conroe ISD and typically feed into newer campuses opened in the past five years.

relatedDistricts.name: Houston Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Texas's largest district (190,000+ students), serving Houston proper and parts of Harris County. Conroe ISD and Houston ISD set calendars independently; Spring Break dates and first-day timing differ most years. Families coordinating travel across the two districts — common in split-custody situations and blended households — should verify both calendars each year rather than assuming alignment.

relatedDistricts.name: Klein Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Klein ISD serves communities immediately south of Conroe ISD along the FM 2920 and Spring Cypress corridors. The two districts share a border, and some neighborhoods near the Conroe ISD-Klein ISD line have addresses in one district while appearing to be in the other by geography. Klein ISD sets its calendar independently of Conroe ISD — Spring Break dates sometimes align and sometimes differ by a week.

relatedDistricts.name: Spring Independent School District
relatedDistricts.comparisonNote: Spring ISD serves communities south of Conroe ISD along the I-45 and FM 1960 corridors. Families in the Spring area should confirm their address falls in Conroe ISD or Spring ISD using each district's school finder, as the boundary runs through some communities. Spring ISD sets its calendar independently and Spring Break dates frequently differ from Conroe ISD.
